callouts.content: SKILL: /ccwork — Onboarding hub and lab runner. Loads lab exercises, creates tracking issues, and sets up starting branches.
SKILL: /view — Opens a file in the system's default GUI viewer for visual inspection outside the terminal.
SKILL: /scpmmr — Full pipeline: Stage → Commit → Push → create MR → Merge. Requires precheck approval before executing.

SKILL: /prepwaves — Analyzes a master issue, validates all sub-issue specs for completeness, computes dependency waves, and prepares for parallel agent execution. This is the planning step before launching wave-based implementation.

The agent reframes the user's capture engine concept as 'schema-on-read instead of schema-on-write' — capture everything raw, classify later. This is a powerful design pattern: don't force structure at ingestion time when you're not sure what matters yet.

Three-layer architecture: Skills for orchestration (user-facing behavior), Hooks for automatic capture (transparent recording), MCP Server for storage (tools + database). Each layer has a distinct responsibility and can evolve independently. This separation is the key design decision of the entire project.

Watch the classifier evolve through three iterations in one conversation: sub-agent spawn → inline classification → Bedrock SDK. Each pivot is driven by a real constraint (API access, latency, complexity). Design is iterative — the first idea is rarely the final architecture.

Three research agents launched in parallel to resolve PRD gaps: SQLite WAL behavior, Claude Code sub-agent APIs, and settings.json schema. This is the agent equivalent of 'I need to look three things up before I can write this section' — parallel research beats sequential.

The user mentions Bedrock access exists, triggering a major design pivot from CLI subprocess to SDK-based classifier. This cascades through constraints, requirements, stories, CI pipeline, and technology table. One new fact can reshape an entire design — this is why PRD review matters.

Running code review on a PRD document — not just code. The reviewer found 9 issues including VRTM inconsistencies, invalid SQLite syntax, and wrong SDK package names. Quality gates apply to all artifacts, not just source code.

wtf_freshell: 'fresh hell' jammed together, officially 'free shell.' Naming decisions like this seem cosmetic but they make tools memorable and fun to use. A tool nobody remembers the name of is a tool nobody uses.

artifacts.title: WTF Server: Three-Layer Architecture
artifacts.description: The architecture that emerged from the design workshop
artifacts.content:
## Three-Layer Architecture

| Layer | Component | Responsibility |
|-------|-----------|---------------|
| **Orchestration** | `/wtf` skill | User-facing commands, mode switching |
| **Capture** | PostToolUse hook | Automatic tool call recording |
| **Storage** | MCP Server | SQLite DB, classification, timeline generation |

### MCP Tools
- `wtf_now` — Manual journal entry
- `wtf_freshell` — Clear incident state
- `wtf_happened` — Generate distilled timeline

### Key Design Decisions
- Schema-on-read: capture raw, classify later
- Single incident model (one at a time)
- Bedrock SDK for background classification
- `.wtf/` directory in project root for state

artifacts.title: Wave Execution Plan
artifacts.content:
## Wave Plan (11 issues across 5 waves)

| Wave | Theme | Issues | Parallel? |
|------|-------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Foundation | #11 (scaffold + DB + CI) | Solo |
| 2 | Core Tools | #12 (wtf_now), #13 (wtf_freshell), #14 (wtf_happened) | 3-way |
| 3 | Automation | #15 (hook+queue), #16 (classifier) | 2-way |
| 4 | Integration | #17 (runbook), #18 (skills), #19 (install), #20 (docs) | 4-way |
| 5 | Verification | #21 (manual testing) | Solo |

**Total: 81 tests across all waves**
